1.4: Saint Ephrem, Saint Jerome, and a great many commentators saw in these insects only a symbol of the pagan peoples: Assyrians, Medes, Persians, and Romans. But many modern scholars understand this invasion literally, relying primarily on the fact that the Prophet speaks only of the damage caused to the fields and the harm done to animals, not to people, whereas, if it had been a war, people would have suffered greatly, and Joel could not have avoided speaking of their tribulations. Moreover, all his words seem to refer to a past event, not a future one. The two opinions can be reconciled by admitting that Joel, in the second part of his Gospel, considers the historical invasion he spoke of in the first part as a type of God's approaching judgment.
1.6: a people ; the locusts, which ravage and destroy everything.
1.8: The Sabians They may be those who lived deep in Arabia Felix.

1.20: «In our time,» says Saint Jerome, “we have seen swarms of locusts cover the land of Judea. When the wind arose, they were driven into the [Dead] Sea and the [Mediterranean] Sea. And as the shores of both seas were covered with the corpses of the dead locusts, which the waves had cast up, the result was a corruption and a stench so harmful that the air was corrupted and produced a plague that struck domestic animals and men.”

1.19: Egypt was indeed devastated by Cambyses, then by Artaxerxes Ochus, and finally by Antiochus Epiphanes. — the Maccabees reduced Idumea to a terrible state (

2.1: Blow the horn in Zion and sound the trumpet on my holy mountain. Let all the inhabitants of the land tremble, for the day of the Lord is coming, for it is near.
2.2: A day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and dark clouds. Like the dawn spreading over the mountains, a people is coming, numerous and strong, such as there has never been since the beginning and will not be after them, until the years of the most distant ages.
2.3: Before him the fire devours and behind him the flame burns. The land is like the Garden of Eden before him and behind him, a desolate desert: nothing escapes him.
2.4: Its appearance is like that of horses, and they run like riders.
2.5: We hear a sound like chariots leaping over mountain peaks; it is like the sound of a flame of fire devouring stubble; it is like a robust people drawn up in battle array.
2.6: Before him, the people tremble with fear, all faces turn pale.
2.7: They run like heroes; they scale the wall like warriors; they each walk in their own paths, they do not confuse their roads.
2.8: They do not push one another, each goes his own way; they rush through the arrows and they do not break ranks.
2.9: They spread throughout the city, they run along the wall, they climb into the houses; they enter through the windows, like a thief.
2.10: Before him the earth trembles, the heavens shake, the sun and moon darken, the stars lose their brightness.
2.11: The Lord raises his voice at the head of his army, for his camp is immense, for mighty is the one who executes his word. For the day of the Lord is great and very dreadful, and who can endure it?
2.12: But even now, declares the Lord, return to me with all your heart, with fasting, with tears and with mourning.
2.13: Rend your hearts and not your garments, and return to the Lord your God, for he is merciful and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love, and he grieves over the calamity he sends.
2.14: Who knows if he will not return and repent and leave behind him a blessing, an offering and a drink offering for the Lord our God?
2.15: Blow the trumpet in Zion, proclaim a fast, call a meeting.
2.16: Assemble the people, proclaim a holy convocation, gather the elders, and bring together the children and infants at their breasts. Let the bridegroom leave his chamber and the bride her bridal canopy.
2.17: Let the priests, ministers of the Lord, weep between the portico and the altar and say: «Lord, spare your people and do not make your inheritance a reproach, an object of scorn to the nations. Why should they say among the peoples, »Where is their God?’”
2.18: The Lord was moved with jealousy for his country and he had compassion on his people.
2.19: The Lord answered and said to his people: Behold, I will send you grain, new wine and oil, and you will be satisfied with them, and I will no longer make you a reproach among the nations.
2.20: I will drive the one who comes from the north far from you and will drive him into a dry and desolate land, the vanguard toward the eastern sea, the rearguard toward the western sea; from there will arise a foul stench and a stench, for he has done great things.
2.21: Earth, do not be afraid, be glad and rejoice, for the Lord has done great things.
2.22: Do not be afraid, beasts of the field, for the pastures of the desert have turned green again, for the tree bears its fruit, the fig tree and the vine yield their riches.
2.23: And you, children of Zion, be glad and rejoice in the Lord your God, for he has given you a teacher to instruct you in righteousness, and he sends down showers for you, autumn rains and spring rains, as before.
2.24: Your barns will be filled with wheat and the vats will overflow with new wine and oil.
2.25: I will compensate you for the years devoured by the grasshopper, the locust, the cricket and the caterpillar, my great army, which I had sent against you.
2.26: You will eat abundantly and be satisfied, and you will praise the name of the Lord your God, who has done marvelous things for you, and my people will never again be put to shame.
2.27: And you will know that I am in the midst of Israel. I am the Lord, your God, and there is no other, and my people will never again be put to shame.

3.1: And after this, I will pour out my Spirit on all people. Your sons and daughters will prophesy, your old men will dream dreams, your young men will see visions.
3.2: Even on the male and female servants, in those days, I will pour out my spirit.
3.3: I will show wonders in the heavens and on the earth, blood and fire and columns of smoke.
3.4: The sun will turn into darkness and the moon into blood, before the coming of the great and terrible day of the Lord.
3.5: And whoever calls on the name of the Lord will be saved, for on Mount Zion and on Jerusalem there will be a gathering of the saved, as the Lord has said, and among the survivors whom the Lord calls.

4.1: For behold, in those days and at that time, when I restore the fortunes of Judah and Jerusalem,
4.2: I will gather all the nations and bring them down to the Valley of Jehoshaphat, and there I will enter into judgment with them concerning my people and my heritage of Israel, whom they scattered among the nations, and my land, which they divided up.
4.3: For they cast lots on my people; they gave the young boy for a prostitute and they sold the young girl for wine and drank.
4.4: And what are you to me, Tyre and Sidon and all the districts of Philistia? Would you seek revenge against me? If you provoke me, I will quickly, swiftly bring your provocation back upon your own heads.,
4.5: You who have taken my silver and my gold and carried off my most precious jewels to your temples,
4.6: you who sold the children of Judah and the children of Jerusalem to the sons of Javan, so that they would be driven away from their land.
4.7: I will make them rise from the place where you sold them and I will make your provocation fall back on your head.
4.8: I will sell your sons and daughters into the hands of the children of Judah, who will sell them to the Sabeans, to a distant people, for the Lord has spoken.
4.9: Proclaim this among the nations: Prepare for war. Raise up the valiant. Let all the fighting men approach, let them come up.
4.10: Beat your plowshares into swords and your sickles into spears; let the weak say, "I am brave."«
4.11: Hasten and come, all you surrounding nations, and gather together. There, Lord, send down your brave men.
4.12: Let the nations arise and come up to the Valley of Jehoshaphat. For there I will sit to judge all the surrounding nations.
4.13: Put in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe; come, tread, for the winepress is full; the vats overflow, for their wickedness is great.
4.14: Crowds, crowds in the valley of decision.
4.15: The sun and moon grew dark and the stars lost their brightness.
4.16: From Zion the Lord will roar, from Jerusalem he will utter his voice; the heavens and the earth will tremble. But the Lord will be a refuge for his people, a stronghold for the children of Israel.
4.17: And you will know that I am the Lord your God, who dwells in Zion, my holy mountain; Jerusalem will be a sanctuary, and foreigners will no longer pass through it.
4.18: On that day the mountains will drip with new wine, the hills with milk, and all the streams of Judah will run with water. A spring will flow from the house of the Lord and water the Valley of Acacias.
4.19: Egypt will become a desolate land, Edom a desolate desert, because of the violence committed against the children of Judah, because they shed innocent blood in their land.
4.20: But Judah will be inhabited forever, and Jerusalem from generation to generation.
4.21: And I will wash away their blood, which I have not yet washed away. And the Lord will dwell in Zion.
